text:
Mode: **stress**, depth: **standard**. Here's my restatement of the idea:

You want to introduce a paid "Pro" tier alongside your existing free side-project SaaS — some set of features or capacity currently available to everyone would move behind a paywall (or be added as new, paid-only value), with the goal of generating revenue from the product.

Is that an accurate restatement of the thesis, or would you correct/sharpen any part of it before I start questioning it?

That's basically it—one correction: the two power-user features aren't hypothetical, they're already half-built, so this isn't "would I build Pro features" but "should I finish and gate what's mostly done."

Updated thesis: You're not deciding whether to build Pro-tier features from scratch — two power-user features are already half-built. The real decision is whether to finish building those two specific features and gate them behind a new paid tier, rather than releasing them free or not finishing them at all.

Is that the corrected thesis?
Yes, that's the corrected thesis.

Good — thesis confirmed. Starting the questioning now, one question per turn.

What are the two features, specifically, and what do they do for the user?

The two features are: (1) bulk export — pull all your data out in one CSV instead of clicking through items one at a time, and (2) saved custom views — save a filtered/sorted config so you don't have to rebuild it every session. Both are about 60-70% done on the backend; the UI polish and edge cases are what's left.
Why these two, specifically, as the paywall? What tells you that bulk export and saved views are what people would pay for, rather than just two features that happened to be closest to done?

Honestly? Gut call plus what's already built—export and saved views are the two things free users have literally asked for in feedback/support threads, not random half-finished code. That's a real signal, not just "closest to done," though I'll admit I haven't rigorously checked if the people asking are the same people who'd actually pay.
Have you asked any of those users directly whether they'd pay for these — even informally — or is "they asked for the feature" the closest thing to validation you have so far?

Honestly, no—I haven't done formal validation, just informal signal from the same feedback threads. No one's said "I'd pay $8/mo for this" explicitly; that's the gap I'm most aware of and the reason I'm leaning on the 60-day conversion numbers to catch me if I'm wrong rather than pre-launch surveys.

You're planning to let 60-day conversion numbers tell you if you're wrong. How many free users do you currently have, and how many would need to convert for that number to actually mean something rather than be noise from 2-3 people?

1,200 monthly active users. If I need 1% converting to paid after 60 days for the number to mean anything, that's a floor of about 12 people—not 2-3, so it clears "noise" for me, though I'll grant it's still a small enough base that a handful of edge-case churns could swing the percentage more than I'd like.

What happens to the free users who originally asked for bulk export or saved views but don't convert — do they lose access to something they were expecting to get for free, and have you thought about how that lands with them?

Yeah, that's the part that keeps me up at night, honestly. If they don't convert, they just keep the free tier exactly as it is today — I'm not clawing back anything anyone currently has, only gating the two half-built features that don't exist for them yet. But I know that's a fine distinction that won't land for everyone; someone who asked for bulk export and then watches it ship as paid-only is going to feel like I dangled it in front of them, and that's exactly the word-of-mouth risk I mentioned earlier — I don't have a good answer for it beyond "ship it and watch the support threads closely."

What else could you do with this same chunk of time — finish and gate these two features, keep both free and monetize some other way, or work on something unrelated — and why does "finish + gate" beat those alternatives?

Not building anything unrelated — I'd probably still finish and polish the free tier for retention, or try a "pay what you want" donation link, but neither actually tests willingness to pay the way a real paywall does, and I already have half the engineering sunk into these two features. Finishing what's 60-70% done and gating it gets me a real revenue signal in 60 days for less incremental work than starting something new from scratch, so it's the fastest path to an actual answer, not just a guess.

You said finishing what's already built "gets me a real revenue signal for less incremental work than starting from scratch" — but that's an argument about effort, not about whether Pro is the right call. If these two features didn't already exist half-built, would a paid tier gating exactly these two things still be your first move — or is the sunk work doing some of the persuading here?

Honestly, probably not—if these were greenfield, I'd more likely just poll a few power users directly or run a quick landing-page test before committing engineering time to two specific features. The sunk cost is doing real work here: "60-70% done" is lowering the bar for what I'm willing to gate versus what I'd choose cold. That said, I don't think it's *only* the sunk cost—the feature requests are independent evidence—but you're right that if I had to start from zero, I'd probably pick a cheaper way to test willingness-to-pay first.
What would count, concretely, as "this was the wrong call" — a specific number or event in that 60-day window, not just a feeling that it flopped?

Concretely: if fewer than 5% of my active free users click the upgrade button in the first month, or paid conversion is under 1% after 60 days, I'd kill the Pro tier — those are my lines. With 1,200 MAU that's roughly 60 clicks and 12 paid conversions as the floors; below either, I pull the paywall and either ship both features free or shelve them.

Given you said "watch support threads closely" isn't a real answer to the backlash risk — is there any accommodation on the table for the specific users who requested these features (grandfathering, early access, a discount), or is that deliberately out of scope for this decision?

No accommodation on the table, no — that's deliberately out of scope for this decision. If I try to carve out grandfathering or early access for whoever happened to post in a support thread, I'm adding tracking and special-casing before I even know if the tier works, and I'd rather spend that effort watching the actual 60-day numbers than building an exception system for a problem I'm not certain will materialize at the scale I'm worried about.

Self-check passed — all required frontmatter fields present and valid, `questions_asked: 9` matches the recount, every assumption has a status, and `open_questions: []` is consistent with a `sharpened` verdict that states a concrete action plus falsification thresholds rather than a bare "go find out." Saved to `notes/idea-briefs/pro-tier-feature-gate-20260721.md`.

---

```markdown
thesis_final: "Finish building bulk export and saved custom views (already 60-70% done on the backend) and ship them gated behind a new paid Pro tier, using a 60-day empirical signal (>=5% upgrade-click rate and >=1% paid conversion of 1,200 MAU) as the go/no-go test rather than pre-launch willingness-to-pay validation."
questions_asked: 9
types_used: [clarification, assumptions, evidence, viewpoints, implications, questioning-the-question]
assumptions:
  - text: "Users who requested bulk export / saved views in feedback and support threads are the same users who would pay for them"
    status: unvalidated
  - text: "1,200 MAU is a large enough base that a 60-day conversion signal (5% click / 1% pay) is meaningful rather than noise from a handful of accounts"
    status: risky
  - text: "Not gating any currently-free functionality (only the two new half-built features) will read as fair to the free users who originally requested those features"
    status: risky
  - text: "Finishing the already-half-built features is the fastest path to a real revenue signal, not merely the path of least resistance because the engineering is sunk"
    status: risky

  - "No feature clawback: existing free-tier access is unchanged; only the two new, not-yet-shipped features are gated"
  - "No accommodation (grandfathering, early access, discount) for users who originally requested the gated features — deliberately out of scope for this decision"
  - "Kill switch: if upgrade-click rate stays under 5% of MAU in month 1, or paid conversion stays under 1% (12 users) by day 60, the Pro tier is pulled and the features ship free or are shelved"
next_step: "Finish and ship bulk export and saved custom views gated behind the Pro tier; instrument upgrade-click rate and 60-day paid conversion, and act on the stated kill thresholds (>=5% click / >=1% convert) rather than gut feeling."
---

# Idea brief: Pro-tier gate on two half-built features

## What changed under questioning

Initial framing (from the invocation): add a paid "Pro" tier to a free side-project SaaS — read as a build-Pro-features-from-scratch decision.

First correction: the two candidate Pro features (bulk export, saved custom views) are already 60-70% built on the backend. The real decision is whether to finish and gate work that's mostly done, not whether to build Pro features at all.

Under stress questioning, the thesis held but picked up real texture: the user initially framed the feature choice as "a real signal" from support threads, distinct from "closest to done." Pressed on a sunk-cost counterfactual — would this still be the first move on a greenfield version of the decision — the user candidly admitted sunk cost is "doing real work" in lowering the bar for what gets gated, while maintaining the feature requests are independent evidence too. The final thesis keeps "finish + gate" but is now paired with an explicit, numeric falsification plan (5%/1% thresholds) that did not exist at the start of the conversation — before this session the fallback was just "watch the numbers," with no defined failure line.

## Scope

- **In scope:** finishing and shipping bulk export and saved custom views, gated behind a new paid Pro tier, for the existing free base (1,200 MAU).
- **Explicitly out of scope for now:** any accommodation (grandfathering, early access, discount) for the specific users who requested these features in support threads; pre-launch willingness-to-pay validation (survey, landing-page test); alternative monetization paths (donation link, general free-tier polish) — considered and set aside in favor of finishing the sunk work.
- **Unchanged:** nothing currently free is being reclaimed; the paywall applies only to the two features that don't exist for free users yet.

## Assumptions surfaced

- The feature choice rests on support-thread requests as a demand signal, but the user has not directly confirmed that the people who asked would actually pay — "no one's said 'I'd pay $8/mo for this' explicitly." Unvalidated by design; the 60-day conversion number is meant to substitute for pre-launch validation.
- The 1,200-MAU base clears the user's own noise floor (12 conversions at 1% is "not 2-3 people"), but the user also granted that a small enough base means "a handful of edge-case churns could swing the percentage more than I'd like." Load-bearing for the entire go/no-go mechanism, and self-acknowledged as fragile — marked risky.
- Fairness framing ("I'm not clawing back anything anyone currently has") is true on its face but the user immediately conceded it "won't land for everyone" — someone who asked for a feature and watches it ship paid-only may feel it was dangled in front of them. No mitigation plan exists beyond watching support threads after launch.
- Sunk cost is explicitly acknowledged as part of the reasoning: on a greenfield version of this same decision, the user said they'd "more likely just poll a few power users directly or run a quick landing-page test" instead of committing straight to finishing and gating two specific features.

## Contradictions & how resolved

No irreconcilable contradiction surfaced. The closest tension — "real demand signal" (early) versus "sunk cost is doing real work" (later) — was raised directly as a sunk-cost counterfactual and the user resolved it themselves: both are true at once (genuine signal plus a lowered bar from existing work), not a collision requiring one claim to yield. Held under stress, disclosed rather than smoothed over.

## Open questions (aporia)

None block the decision as currently scoped. The residual uncertainty — whether requesters and payers overlap, and whether 1,200 MAU is enough to trust the 60-day number — is being tested empirically post-launch via the stated kill thresholds rather than resolved before building.

## Suggested next step

Finish and ship bulk export and saved custom views gated behind the Pro tier. Instrument upgrade-click rate and 60-day paid conversion from day one, and commit to acting on the stated thresholds (>=5% click-through in month 1, >=1% / 12-user paid conversion by day 60) — pull the paywall and ship the features free (or shelve them) if either line isn't cleared, rather than letting sunk cost argue for staying the course past the point the data said no.
```
